title: Multicast Traffic Monitoring
spl:
index=network sourcetype="netflow"
| where cidrmatch("224.0.0.0/4", dest)
| stats sum(bytes) as total_bytes, dc(src) as sources by dest
| eval MB=round(total_bytes/1048576,1) | sort -total_bytes
| head 20
description: Uncontrolled multicast traffic floods switches and consumes bandwidth. Monitoring ensures multicast storms are detected before impacting unicast traffic.
value: Uncontrolled multicast traffic floods switches and consumes bandwidth. Monitoring ensures multicast storms are detected before impacting unicast traffic.
implementation: Enable NetFlow on core/distribution switches. Filter for multicast destination range (224.0.0.0/4). Baseline expected multicast groups. Alert on new or high-volume groups.
visualization: Table (multicast group, volume, sources), Timechart (multicast volume), Bar chart.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(All_Traffic.bytes_in) as bytes_in sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out dc(All_Traffic.src) as sources
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.dest span=1h
| eval total_bytes=bytes_in+bytes_out
| where cidrmatch("224.0.0.0/4", All_Traffic.dest)
| sort -total_bytes
| head 20
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Stream, NetFlow integrator.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=netflow`, `sourcetype=cisco:ios`.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Enable NetFlow on core/distribution switches. Filter for multicast destination range (224.0.0.0/4). Baseline expected multicast groups. Alert on new or high-volume groups.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=network sourcetype="netflow"
| where cidrmatch("224.0.0.0/4", dest)
| stats sum(bytes) as total_bytes, dc(src) as sources by dest
| eval MB=round(total_bytes/1048576,1) | sort -total_bytes
| head 20
```

Understanding this SPL

**Multicast Traffic Monitoring** ‚Äî Uncontrolled multicast traffic floods switches and consumes bandwidth. Monitoring ensures multicast storms are detected before impacting unicast traffic.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=netflow`, `sourcetype=cisco:ios`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Stream, NetFlow integrator. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: network; **sourcetype**: netflow. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=network, sourcetype="netflow". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by dest** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **MB** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
‚Ä¢ Limits the number of rows with `head`.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(All_Traffic.bytes_in) as bytes_in sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out dc(All_Traffic.src) as sources
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.dest span=1h
| eval total_bytes=bytes_in+bytes_out
| where cidrmatch("224.0.0.0/4", All_Traffic.dest)
| sort -total_bytes
| head 20
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Multicast Traffic Monitoring** ‚Äî Uncontrolled multicast traffic floods switches and consumes bandwidth. Monitoring ensures multicast storms are detected before impacting unicast traffic.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=netflow`, `sourcetype=cisco:ios`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Stream, NetFlow integrator. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` ‚Äî enable acceleration for that model.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **bytes** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
On a device that should carry known multicast, compare the top groups in Splunk to `show ip mroute` (or the vendor‚Äôs multicast display) and to NetFlow/Stream for the same window.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (multicast group, volume, sources), Timechart (multicast volume), Bar chart.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see when one-to-many network traffic (like video or some apps) is flooding links so you can fix it before normal calls and web traffic slow down.
dataModelAcceleration: Network_Traffic / All_Traffic ‚Äî enable data model acceleration before using this `tstats` example.
knownFalsePositives: IPTV, trading floors, and imaging can legitimately use heavy multicast. Traffic spikes during backup jobs, large file transfers, or video streaming (including multicast video) are often normal; baseline known groups and PIM changes.
